content.full_text:
June 23, 1977

Mr. John F. Falconer
Secretary/Treasurer
San Diego County Deputy
Sheriffs' Association
2555 Mildred Street
San Diego, California 92110

Dear Mr. Falconer:

Thank you for your letter of June 15 in which you asked several
questions relating to contributions which are collected by law
enforcement associations for the purpose of making campaign
contributions.

The following answers parallel numerically to the questions posed
in your letter.

1. The Political Reform Act of 1974 does not prohibit any
organization, association, groups of individuals, etc.
from collecting money for the purpose of making
political contributions. If dues are collected by your
association, only that portion of the dues which are
earmarked (set aside) for political purposes need be
reported. For example, if the monthly membership dues
are $20.00, $3.00 of which is earmarked for political
purposes, only that portion ($3.00) need be reported.

Pursuant to Section 82013 of the Act, a committee is
defined as "any person or combination of persons who
directly or indirectly receives contributions or makes
expenditures or contributions for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence the action of
the voters for or against the nomination or election
of one or more candidates, or the passage or defeat of
any measure, including any committee or subcommittee
of a political party, whether national, state or local
if:

(a) Contributions received total five hundred dollars
or more in a calendar year;

John F. Falconer                    -2-                    June 23, 1977


     (b) Expenditures and contributions made, other than con-
tributions described in subsection (c), total five
hundred dollars or more in a calendar year; or

     (c) Contributions of cash, checks and other cash equiva-
lents paid directly to candidates and committees
total five thousand or more in a calendar year."

Because your association receives monies for the purpose
of making political contributions, it is a Type A committee
or what is more commonly referred to as a "Recipient
Committee." It will qualify as a Recipient Committee as
soon as it has raised $500. Upon reaching the $500 level,
it must, within 10 days, file a Statement of Organization
(Form 410) with the Secretary of State's office. The
Secretary of State will issue the committee an identification
number which must be used when filing campaign statements.

2.    There is no limit on the amount of contributions that can
be made to a candidate under state law, however, some
cities have local ordinances which do restrict the amount of
contributions which may be given to each candidate.

3.    No. As stated in number one above, the only reportable
portion of the dues collected by the association is that
amount earmarked for political purposes.

4.    If a political action committee is supporting federal
candidates and filing pursuant to the Federal Elections
Commission's guidelines and it also contributes to a
candidate(s) running in a state or local election, the
political action committee will have a filing obligation
under the Political Reform Act. Federal guidelines do
apply to elections conducted and contributions made
to state and local candidates in California.

Enclosed are several fact sheets and a new campaign disclosure manual
which should be of assistance to you and your association.

                                   Sincerely,


                                   Mary Ann Kvasager
                                   Technical Assistance and
                                   Analysis Division
Enclosures

77912

SHERIFFS
San Diego County Deputy Sheriffs Association

5555 MILDRED STREET
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92110
TELEPHONE 291-2570

June 15, 1977

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K St. Bldg.
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Sir:

In order to clear up a confusion on political contributions
regarding our Association, your assistance is requested by
answering the following questions.

1. Can local police and Deputy Sheriff's associations donate
monies collected from individuals as dues for purposes of
campaign contributions?

2. If so, is there a limit on the amount of contributions
to each candidate?

3. Does the dues monies have to be collected from the membership
for the specific purpose of campaign contributions?

4. Do Federal Political Action guidelines as far as contributions
to candidates are concerned also apply to how funds are raised
and contributed in state campaigns?

I would appreciate your immediate attention to these questions if at
all possible.

Thanking you for your consideration.

[signature]
John F. Falconer, Secretary/Treasurer
S.D.C.D.S.A.

JFF/pja

No. As stated in number one above, the only reportable portion of the dues collected by the association is that amount earmarked for political purposes. 4.
